author: Council of Ephesus
work: Cyril's Second Letter to Nestorius (ratified at Ephesus, 431)
section: christological core (the incarnation and the one Christ)
note: The Third Ecumenical Council (431) did not issue a fresh creed; it ratified Cyril of Alexandria's Second Letter to Nestorius as the standard of orthodoxy against Nestorianism (which was heard to divide Christ into two persons/subjects). This is the christological heart of that letter: the Word did not change into flesh, but the two natures are 'brought together in a true union' so that there is 'one Christ and one Son' without the difference of the natures being taken away — the same logic Chalcedon 451 would formalize. Denominationally neutral (Catholic/Orthodox/Protestant common ground). verified:false — quoted from the Percival NPNF text via New Advent; awaiting exact-wording confirmation by apologia-citations against the CCEL/NPNF leaf page. DELICACY: "the two natures being brought together in a true union" reads Nestorian/adoptionist (two things merely joined) if lifted alone — it must always travel with its resolving clause, "there is of both one Christ and one Son." Related: "neither on account of taking to himself a person" uses "person" in Nestorius's technical sense (prosopon), NOT "a human being" — worth an orthonote clarifier if ever quoted in card-length format. NOTE: this passage is byte-identical to cyril-2nd-letter-nestorius-1 (Ephesus ratified Cyril's letter rather than issuing a fresh creed); both are retained on record, but a retrieval layer may double-hit them. Verified verbatim against the printed NPNF2-14 (Percival) via tertullian.org, cross-checked on independent hosts (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20). TRANSLATION PURITY: liturgies.net and papalencyclicals.net carry a MODERN (Tanner-family) translation of this same text ("So we shall confess one Christ and one Lord") and must never be cited for this entry — Percival reads "We, therefore, confess one Christ and Lord."
text: For we do not say that the nature of the Word was changed and became flesh, or that it was converted into a whole man consisting of soul and body; but rather that the Word having personally united to himself flesh animated by a rational soul, did in an ineffable and inconceivable manner become man, and was called the Son of Man, not merely as willing or being pleased to be so called, neither on account of taking to himself a person, but because the two natures being brought together in a true union, there is of both one Christ and one Son; for the difference of the natures is not taken away by the union, but rather the divinity and the humanity make perfect for us the one Lord Jesus Christ by their ineffable and inexpressible union.

author: Council of Ephesus
work: Cyril's Second Letter to Nestorius (ratified at Ephesus, 431)
section: on calling the holy Virgin 'Theotokos' (Mother of God)
note: DELICACY / denominational-neutrality note: 'Theotokos' ('God-bearer' / 'Mother of God') is a CHRISTOLOGICAL term, not a Marian-elevation doctrine. Its whole point — stated in this very passage — is that Mary's divinity did NOT begin in her ('not as if the nature of the Word or his divinity had its beginning from the Holy Virgin'); rather, the one she bore in the flesh is God the Son, personally united to that human body from conception. So the term safeguards the UNITY of Christ's one person against Nestorianism: the baby of Bethlehem is the eternal Word, not a mere man to whom the Word was later attached. On that christological point — that the one born of Mary is truly God — Catholics, Eastern Orthodox, and Protestants agree; Reformers including Luther and Calvin affirmed 'Theotokos' in exactly this sense. This entry is NOT about later Marian devotional doctrines (which ARE disputed between traditions and are outside this library's neutral scope). verified:false — quoted from the Percival NPNF text via New Advent; awaiting exact-wording confirmation by apologia-citations. CORRECTED 2026-07-20: "Holy Virgin" -> "holy Virgin" (twice). Percival prints an asymmetry — lowercase "holy Virgin" but capitalised "Mother of God" — preserved here exactly. The phrase "they ventured to call" is confirmed correct as printed. DELICACY: the Theotokos title is a CHRISTOLOGICAL guarantee about the identity of the child, not a Marian-elevation doctrine; its own resolving qualifier is inside the quote — "not as if the nature of the Word or his divinity had its beginning from the holy Virgin." Verified verbatim against the printed NPNF2-14 (Percival) via tertullian.org, cross-checked on independent hosts (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: This was the sentiment of the holy Fathers; therefore they ventured to call the holy Virgin, the Mother of God, not as if the nature of the Word or his divinity had its beginning from the holy Virgin, but because of her was born that holy body with a rational soul, to which the Word being personally united is said to be born according to the flesh.

author: Cyril of Alexandria and John of Antioch
work: The Formula of Reunion (433), from Cyril's Letter to John of Antioch ('Laetentur coeli')
section: the reconciling confession of the two natures in one Christ — printed in NPNF2-14 under the Council of CHALCEDON (read at Session II), though Percival notes it is often styled "the Ephesine Creed"
note: After the bitter clash at Ephesus, Cyril of Alexandria and John of Antioch reconciled in 433 on this shared confession (drafted largely by the Antiochene party), which harmonized Alexandrian 'one Christ' language with Antiochene 'two natures' language and became a key bridge-text to the Chalcedonian Definition of 451. Note 'of the same substance with us according to his humanity' — a strong affirmation of Christ's FULL humanity. Denominationally neutral. verified:false — quoted from the Percival NPNF text via New Advent; awaiting exact-wording confirmation by apologia-citations. CITATION NOTE (corrected 2026-07-20): in NPNF2-14 this Letter of Cyril to John of Antioch is printed under CHALCEDON, not Ephesus — a reader checking the citation in the Ephesus chapter will not find it. DELICACY: "begotten before the ages of the Father according to his Divinity" is origin/taxis language, resolved within the same sentence by "before the ages" and "of the same substance with his Father"; safe as stored provided it is never cut mid-sentence. Verified verbatim against the printed NPNF2-14 (Percival) via tertullian.org, cross-checked on independent hosts (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20). TRANSLATION PURITY: liturgies.net and papalencyclicals.net carry a MODERN (Tanner-family) translation of this same text ("So we shall confess one Christ and one Lord") and must never be cited for this entry — Percival reads "We, therefore, confess one Christ and Lord."
text: We confess, therefore, our Lord Jesus Christ, the Only Begotten Son of God, perfect God, and perfect Man of a reasonable soul and flesh consisting; begotten before the ages of the Father according to his Divinity, and in the last days, for us and for our salvation, of Mary the Virgin according to his humanity, of the same substance with his Father according to his Divinity, and of the same substance with us according to his humanity; for there became a union of two natures. Wherefore we confess one Christ, one Son, one Lord.

author: Second Council of Constantinople
work: The Capitula (Anathemas) of the Fifth Ecumenical Council (Constantinople II)
note: The Fifth Ecumenical Council (553) endorsed the 'theopaschite' formula — 'one of the Holy Trinity suffered/was crucified in the flesh' (Latin: Unus ex Trinitate passus est carne). DELICACY: this is orthodox, and it is NOT patripassianism (the heresy that the FATHER suffered). It is the communicatio idiomatum ('exchange of properties'): because the one crucified is a single divine person — God the Son — one may truly say that God the Son suffered, precisely 'in the flesh' / according to His human nature, without the divine nature itself changing or the Father or the Spirit suffering. Cf. our fenced treatment of patripassianism elsewhere (e.g. the Moltmann note in evil.html): the divine Son genuinely suffered in His humanity; the divine nature is impassible. Denominationally neutral. verified:false — Canon X quoted from the Percival NPNF text via New Advent; awaiting exact-wording confirmation by apologia-citations (some editions number the anathemas differently). CITATION NOTE (corrected 2026-07-20): NPNF2-14 heads this series "The Capitula of the Council," numbered X — it is NOT "Canon X" (the Fifth Council's canons are a separate series), and mislabelling it invites a skeptic's correction. The entry's earlier uncertainty about numbering is resolved: it is X in NPNF2-14. Percival prints the first word as a small-cap "IF"; storing "If" is normal normalisation, not an error. DELICACY: "one of the Holy Trinity" crucified carries a patripassian misread risk; the resolving clause "who was crucified in the flesh" is already inside the stored text and must never be trimmed. Verified verbatim against the printed NPNF2-14 (Percival) via tertullian.org, cross-checked on independent hosts (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: If anyone does not confess that our Lord Jesus Christ who was crucified in the flesh is true God and the Lord of Glory and one of the Holy Trinity: let him be anathema.

author: Third Council of Constantinople
work: The Definition of Faith of the Sixth Ecumenical Council (680-681)
section: two natural wills in Christ (dyothelitism, against Monothelitism)
note: The Sixth Ecumenical Council (680-681) condemned Monothelitism (the teaching that the incarnate Christ has only one will) and defined dyothelitism: because Christ has two complete natures (Chalcedon), He has two natural wills and two natural operations — a fully human will alongside His divine will. The human will is not overridden or absorbed; it 'follows,' freely and without resistance, His divine will. This safeguards Christ's FULL, unabridged humanity (a genuine human will that could pray 'not my will, but thine be done,' Luke 22:42) while preserving the harmony of the one person. Denominationally neutral. DELIBERATE OMISSION: this library intentionally does NOT include the Second Council of Nicaea (787) on the veneration of images — the status/authority of that council and image-veneration are genuinely disputed between Protestant, Orthodox, and Catholic traditions, so it falls outside our denominational-neutrality scope. verified:false — quoted from the Percival NPNF text via New Advent and cross-checked against a second host (Christian History Institute); the opening clause and adverb list ('indivisibly, inconvertibly, inseparably, inconfusedly') still await exact-wording confirmation by apologia-citations against the CCEL/NPNF leaf page. CORRECTED 2026-07-20 (two verbatim defects): the entry silently capitalised a mid-sentence participial clause into "We likewise declare" — restored to the printed "defining all this we likewise declare"; and a spurious comma after "follows" was removed ("his human will follows and that not as resisting"). The adverb list "indivisibly, inconvertibly, inseparably, inconfusedly" is CONFIRMED exactly as stored. DELICACY — HIGHEST RISK IN THIS FILE: "his human will follows... as subject to his divine and omnipotent will" is, lifted alone, a textbook subordinationist pull-quote reading as the Son subject to the Father. It is not: the subjection is INTRA-CHRIST — Christ's human will to Christ's OWN divine will. HARD RULE: never excerpt the "subject to" clause without "in him are two natural wills and two natural operations" ahead of it and "these two natural wills are not contrary the one to the other" with it; and never drop the word "will" from either side, since the possessive "his divine and omnipotent will" is what makes it safe. SOURCING CAVEAT: this entry rests on three agreeing secondary NPNF mirrors — the CCEL/tertullian leaf for the Sixth Council's Definition did not resolve (both candidate URLs serve the Canons in Trullo) and archive.org's scan was unreachable. A future session with archive.org available should confirm against the printed page. Verified verbatim against the printed NPNF2-14 (Percival) via tertullian.org, cross-checked on independent hosts (apologia-citations, web, 2026-07-20).
text: defining all this we likewise declare that in him are two natural wills and two natural operations indivisibly, inconvertibly, inseparably, inconfusedly, according to the teaching of the holy Fathers. And these two natural wills are not contrary the one to the other (God forbid!) as the impious heretics assert, but his human will follows and that not as resisting and reluctant, but rather as subject to his divine and omnipotent will.
